questions.question_text: Among integers 1 to 300, how many of them are divisible neither by 3, nor by 5, nor by 7? How many of them are divisible by 3 but not by 5, nor by 7?
Prove by mathematical induction: $$1 + 4 + 7 + ... + (3n - 2) = 2n (3n - 1)$$

Prove :
$$i) A \times (B \cap C) = (A \times B) \cap (A \times C)$$
$$ii) A \times (B \cup C) = (A \times B) \cup (A \times C)$$
Explain Pigeonhole principle with an example.
Show that the mapping $$f : R \rightarrow R, f(x) = \frac{1}{x}, x \neq 0$$ and $$x \in R$$ is one-one onto, where R is the set of non-zero real numbers.
Explain Pigeonhole principle with an example.
Find the number of integers between 1 and 500 that are not divisible by any of the integers 2, 3 and 5.
A briefly explain the application of Pigeon Hole principle using an example.

Prove by mathematical induction.
$$1 + 4 + 7 + ... + (3n - 2) = 2n (3n - 1)$$
Show that the relation 'R' defined by $$(a, b) R (c, d)$$ if $$a + d = b + c$$ is an equivalence relation.
If $$X = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$$ and $$R = \{(x, y)/x < y\}$$. Draw the graph of 'R' and also give its matrix.
Prove that If R is an equivalence relation on a set A, show that $$R^{-1}$$ is also an equivalence relation on A.
What is Mathematical induction? Use mathematical induction to prove that $$1.1! + 2.2! + ... + n.n! = (n + 1)! - 1$$, wherever n is a positive integer.
Explain the method of proving theorems by direct, indirect, contradiction and by cases.

Let $$X = \{a, b, c\}$$. Define $$f : X \rightarrow X$$ such that $$f = \{(a, b), (b, a), (c, c)\}$$ Find
i) $$f^{-1}$$
ii) $$f^2$$
iii) $$f^3$$
iv) $$f^4$$

Among 100 students, 32 study Mathematics, 20 study Physics, 45 study Biology, 15 study Mathematics and Biology, 7 study Mathematics and Physics, 10 study Physics and Biology and 30 do not study any of the subjects. Find the number of students
i) Studying all the three subjects and
ii) Studying exactly one of the three subjects.
Let $$A = \{2, 3, 4\}$$ and $$B = \{3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$$. Assume a relation R from A to B such that $$(x, y) \in R$$ when a divides 6. Determine R, its domain and range.
Types of functions

Out of 120 students surveyed, it was found that 20 students have studied French, 50 students have studied English, 70 students have studied Hindi, 5 have studied English and French, 20 have studied English and Hindi, 10 have studied Hindi and French, only 3 students have studied all the three languages. Find how many students have studied:
i) Hindi alone
ii) French alone
iii) English, but not Hindi
iv) Hindi, but not French
If R be a relation in the set of integers Z defined by $$R = \{(x, y) : x \in Z, y \in Z, (x - y)$$ is multiple of 3 $$\$$. Show that it is an equivalence relation.
Show that $$1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + ... + n^2 = \frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}, n \geq 1$$ by mathematical induction
Prove that the relation R defined by "a is congruent to b modulo m" on the set of integers is an equivalence relation.
Prove that $$5^{2n} - 1$$ is divisible by 24, where n is any positive integer.
Show that the relation 'R' defined by $$(a, b) R (c, d)$$ iff $$a + d = b + c$$ is an equivalence relation.
Define various types of functions. How many symmetric and reflexive relations are possible from a set A containing 'n' elements?
Define Pigeon hole Principle. Write the contra positive of the implication: "if it is Sunday then it is a holiday."
Find the numbers between 1 to 500 that are not divisible by any of the integers 2 or 3 or 5 or 7.

Consider three sets $$A = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}, B = \{3, 4, 5, 6\}$$ and $$C = \{1, 5, 7\}$$. Using Venn diagrams, determine and shade the regions corresponding to $$(A \cup B) \cap (B \cap C)$$ and $$A \cap (B \cup C)$$. Verify if these expressions are equivalent by listing the elements.

Define and illustrate an equivalence relation by constructing an equivalence relation on the set $$S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$$ where two elements are related if they have the same remainder when divided by 3.
What is pigeonhole principle. Prove it by using mathematical induction and use it to show that in a group of 15 people, at least two share the same birth month.
Explain a recursively defined function. Solve the recurrence relation $$f(n) = f(n - 1) + 3$$ with initial condition $$f(0) = 2$$.
Partial Ordering Relation
Countable and uncountable sets
Define countable and uncountable sets. Prove that the set of rational numbers is countable.

Using Venn diagram, prove:
$$A \cap (B \cup C) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C)$$
Define relation. Explain equivalence relation and partial ordering relation with suitable examples.

Let
$$A = \{1, 2, 3\}$$
$$R = \{(1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3), (1, 2), (2, 1)\}$$
Check whether R is an equivalence relation and find equivalence classes.
Define one-one, onto and bijective functions. Prove that inverse of a function exists if and only if the function is bijective.
State and prove Pigeonhole Principle.

Prove by mathematical induction that:
$$1 + 2 + 3 + ..... + n = \frac{n(n + 1)}{2}$$
Prove by contradiction that $$\sqrt{2}$$ is irrational.
Prove that every finite group G is isomorphic to a per mutation group.
Define cyclic group with suitable example.
Prove that the group homomorphism preserves the identity element.
Prove that the set $$G = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$$ is a finite abelian group of order 7 with respect to multiplication modulo 7 as the composition in G.
State the Lagrange's Theorem with example. Also explain Permutation and Symmetric Group.

Consider $$G = \{1, 5, 7, 11\}$$ under multiplication modulo 12
i) Find the order of each element
ii) Is G cyclic
iii) Find all subgroups
Prove that a subgroup H of a group G, is normal if and only if $$xHx^{-1} = H, \forall x \in G$$
Let G be any (additive) abelian group. Define a multiplication in G by $$a * b = 0$$ for every $$a, b \in G$$. Show that this makes G into a ring.
Prove that $$F = \{a + b\sqrt{2} ; a, b$$ rational$$\}$$ is a field.

Define the following
i) Symmetric Group
ii) Normal Subgroup
iii) Homomorphism
Let Z be the group of integers with binary operation * defined by $$a * b = a + b - 2$$, for all $$a, b \in Z$$. Find the identity element of the group <Z, *>.
Show that every Cyclic group is Abelian. Prove that a lattice with 5 elements is not a Boolean algebra.
Prove that $$G = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$$ is an abelian group of order 7 with respect to addition modulo 7.
Prove or disprove that intersection of two normal subgroups of a group G is again a normal subgroup of G. Define subgroup, normal subgroup, Quotient group, with an example for each.
Let Z be the group of integers with binary operation * defined by $$a * b = a + b - 2$$, for all $$a, b \in Z$$. Find the identity element of the group <Z, *>.
Show that every Cyclic group is Abelian. Prove that a lattice with 5 elements is not a Boolean algebra.
What is Ring? Define elementary properties of Ring with example.
Prove or disprove that intersection of two normal subgroups of a group G is again a normal subgroup of G.
Prove that the set $$G = \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$$ under addition modulo 4 is an abelian group. Identify the identity and inverse elements.
Define a homomorphism between two groups. Verify whether the mapping $$f : \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$ defined by $$f(x) = 2x$$ is a homomorphism under addition.
Permutation group
Define group and Abelian group. Prove that identity element of a group is unique.
Find all subgroups of the group $$Z_8$$.

Show that
$$[(p \wedge q) \Rightarrow p] \Rightarrow (q \wedge \sim q)$$ is a contradiction.

Show that the language
$$L = \{a^m : m = i^2, i \geq 1\}$$ is not a finite state

Prove by truth table that the following formula are tautologies
i) $$(\sim q \Rightarrow \sim p) \wedge (q \Rightarrow p) \Rightarrow (p \Leftrightarrow q)$$
ii) $$(p \Leftrightarrow q \wedge r) \Rightarrow (\sim r \Rightarrow \sim p)$$
Universal and existential quantition

Show that the rule of hypothetical syllogism is valid.
$$p \rightarrow q$$
$$q \rightarrow r$$
$$p \rightarrow r$$

Prove that the proposition is a tautology.
$$(p \vee \sim q) \wedge (\sim p \vee \sim q) \vee q$$
Find PDNF by constructing its PCNF of $$(Q \vee P) \wedge (Q \vee R) \wedge (\sim(P \vee R) \vee \sim Q)$$.

Prove that for any three propositions P, Q, R the compound proposition $$(P \rightarrow (Q \rightarrow R)) \rightarrow ((P \rightarrow Q) \rightarrow (P \rightarrow R))$$ is a tautology by laws of logic.
Explain Tautologies, Contradiction and Contingencies with suitable examples.

Prove the validity of the following argument:
"If Ram is selected in IAS examination, then he will not be able to go to London. Since Ram is going to London. He will not be selected in IAS examination."
Design DFA and NDFA accepting all string over $$\{\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{1}\}$$. Which end in 0 but do not contain 11 as substring?

Prove by truth table the following.
i) $$(p \leftrightarrow p \wedge r) \Rightarrow (\sim r \rightarrow \sim p)$$ is tautology
ii) $$(p \vee q) \wedge (\sim p) \wedge (\sim q)$$ is a contradiction
Conjunctive and Disjunctive Normal Form
Universal and existential quantifier

Construct the truth table of the following formula:
i) $$\sim(p \vee (q \vee r)) \Leftrightarrow ((p \vee q) \wedge (p \vee r))$$
ii) $$(\sim q \Rightarrow \sim p) \Rightarrow (p \Rightarrow q)$$

Write the negation of the following.
i) If the determinant of a system of linear equations is zero then either the system has no solution or has an indefinite no. of solutions.
ii) Either today is not a Sunday or today is not a Wednesday.
Show that the proposition $$\neg(p \wedge q)$$ and $$\neg p \vee q$$ are logically equivalent.

Obtain the conjunctive normal form of
i) $$p \wedge (p \Rightarrow q)$$
ii) $$\sim p \Rightarrow [r \wedge (p \Rightarrow q)]$$

i) Prove that $$p \wedge q \Rightarrow q \vee p$$ is a Tautology.
ii) Show that $$(p \vee q) \wedge (\sim p) \wedge (\sim q)$$ is a contradiction.
Explain various Rules of Inference for Propositional Logic.

Prove the validity of the following argument "if the races are fixed so the casinos are crooked, then the tourist trade will decline. If the tourist trade decreases, then the police will be happy. The police force is never happy. Therefore, the races are not fixed."
Explain various Rules of Inference for Propositional Logic.

Consider the propositions p, q and r, where :
p : "It is raining."
q : "I am carrying an umbrella."
r : "I stay dry."
Assume the implication $$(p \rightarrow q) \rightarrow r$$. Construct a truth table for the expression $$(p \rightarrow q) \rightarrow r$$ and determine if it is a tautology or contradiction.
What are predicates in propositional logic? Define universal and existential quantifiers with examples.
Finite state machines as language recognizers

Construct truth table for the proposition.
$$(p \rightarrow q) \leftrightarrow (\neg p \vee q)$$ and show that it is a tautology.
Convert the following statement into predicate logic and write its negation: "Every student studies Discrete Mathematics."
Define Finite State Machine. Explain FSM as a language recognizer.
Design a finite state machine that accepts all binary strings ending with 01.
Find the shortest path of the following graph.
Define chromatic number with diagram.
Graph coloring
Find the shortest path from a to z in the following graph, where numbers associated with the edges are the weights.
Show that a regular binary tree has an odd number of vertices.
Prove that a connected graph G is Euler graph if and only if every vertex of G is of even degree.
What is Graph coloring? Define chromatic number give any one example to explain your answer.
Give a simple condition on the weights of a graph that will guarantee that there is a unique maximal spanning tree for the graph.
Define Isomorphism of graphs. What are the steps followed in discovering the Isomorphism?

Explain Eulerian and Hamiltonian graphs with examples, also draw the graphs of the following:
i) Eulerian but not Hamiltonian
ii) Hamiltonian but not Eulerian
Prove that the sum of the degree of all the vertices in a graph G is equal to twice the number of edges in G.
Find the shortest path from a to z in the following graph, where numbers associated with the edges are the weights.
Distinguish between k-coloring of a graph and chromatic number of a graph.
Prove that, in a graph total number of odd degree vertices is even but then number of even degree vertices may be odd.
Determine whether the graphs F1 and F2 are isomorphic.
Find an Euler Path in the graph below.

Explain the following.
i) Euler Graph
ii) Isomorphic graphs
iii) Minimal spanning tree
iv) Height of the tree
Explain complete digraph and Euler Graph using suitable example of both.
Define planar graph. Prove that for any connected planar graph, $$v - e + r = 2$$ Where v, e, r is the number of vertices, edges, and regions of the graph respectively.
Show that there does not exist a graph with 5 vertices with degrees 1, 3, 4, 2, 3 respectively.
Define planar graph. Prove that for any connected planar graph, $$v - e + r = 2$$ Where v, e, r is the number of vertices, edges, and regions of the graph respectively.
Discuss about Complete digraph and Euler Graph with the help of suitable examples.
Define graph theory and explain the basic terminology of graphs such as vertices, edges, degree and adjacency.
Define and give an example of an isomorphic graph pair. Verify isomorphism between two adjacency matrices.

For the graph G with vertices $$V = \{A, B, C, D\}$$ and edges $$E = \{(A, B), (B, C), (C, D), (D, A), (A, C)\}$$, determine :
i) Whether the graph contains a Eulerian path or circuit or not.
ii) All Hamiltonian circuits in the graph G.
Define Euler path and Euler circuit. State necessary and sufficient conditions for their existence.
Explain Dijkstra's Algorithm and find the shortest path from a given source vertex in a weighted graph.

Solve the recurrence relation
$$a_{n+2} - 6a_{n+1} + 9a_n = 3(2^n) + 7(3^n), n \geq 0$$
given test $$a_0 = 1$$ and $$a_1 = 4$$.
Explain Binomial theorem and prove it. Take any one example to illustrate the theorem.
Properties of Lattice
Multinomial coefficients
Prove that in Boolean algebra $$(a \vee b)' = a' \wedge b'$$.
Draw Hasse diagram for the "less than or equal to" relation on set $$A = \{ 0, 2, 5, 10, 11, 15 \}$$.

Solve the recurrence relation :
$$a_r - 7a_{r-1} + 10a_{r-2} = 0$$ given $$a_0 = 0$$ and $$a_1 = 6$$.
Hasse diagram
Lattices
Binomial theorem
Find the explicit formula for the Fibonacci numbers. Use $$f_n = f_{n-1} + f_{n-2}$$ as recursive condition and $$f_0 = 0$$ and $$f_1 = 1$$ as initial condition.
Draw the Hasse diagram representing the positive divisors of 36.

Solve the following recurrence relation:
$$a_r - 7a_{r-1} + 10a_{r-2} = 3^r$$
given that $$a_0 = 0, a_1 = 1$$
Explain Binomial theorem and prove it. Take any one example to illustrate the theorem.
Lattice

Define a lattice. Let $$(L, \leq, \vee, \wedge)$$ be a lattice, and $$a, b, c, d \in L$$ be such that $$a \leq b$$ and $$c \leq d$$. Show that $$a \vee c \leq b \vee d$$ and $$a \wedge c \leq b \wedge d$$.
Show that $$D_{12}$$ and $$D_{18}$$ are isomorphic lattices. Further, show that none is isomorphic to the lattice $$D_{20}$$.
Show that $$a_n = c_1 2^n + c_2 4^n$$ is a solution of the recurrence relation $$a_n - 6a_{n-1} + 8a_{n-2} = 0$$.
Find the sequence having the generating function $$G(x)$$ given by $$\frac{x}{1 - 2x}$$.
Prove that the Complement of each element in a Boolean Algebra B is unique.

Let A be any finite set and P(A) be the power set of A. $$\subseteq$$ be the inclusion relation on the elements of P(A). Draw the Hasse diagrams of $$(P(A), \subseteq)$$ for the following
i) $$A = \{a\}$$
ii) $$A = \{a, b\}$$
iii) $$A = \{a, b, c\}$$
iv) $$A = \{a, b, c, d\}$$
Draw the Hasse diagram representing the positive divisors of 36 and 45.
Obtain the generating function for the sequence 4, 4, 4, 4, 4, 4. Explain complete digraph.

A collection of 10 electric bulbs contain 3 defective ones (i) In how many ways can a sample of four bulbs be selected? (ii) In how many ways can a sample of 4 bulbs be selected which contain 2 good bulbs and 2 defective ones? (iii) In how many ways can a sample of 4 bulbs be selected so that either the sample contains 3 good ones and 1 defectives ones or 1 good and 3 defectives ones?

Solve the following recurrence equation using generating function.
$$G(K) - 7 G(K-1) + 10 G(K-2) = 8K + 6$$.
Let $$(L, \vee, \wedge, \leq)$$ be a distributive lattice and $$a b, \in L$$. if $$a \wedge b = a \wedge c$$ and $$a \vee b = a \vee c$$ then show that $$b = c$$.
How many permutations can be formed from the letters of the word "MATHEMATICS" such that all vowels are together?
What is a Hasse diagram? Draw the Hasse diagram for the set $$\{\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{2}, \mathbf{3}, \mathbf{6}, \mathbf{9}, \mathbf{18}\}$$ with the divisibility relation.
Define a lattice and prove that every finite lattice has a unique least upper bound and greatest lower bound.
Binomial theorem
